questions.stem: The findings of the report were widely ( ) by the public.
questions.explanation: disseminate findings = 結果を広く伝える/普及させる。discount = 割引、diversify = 多様化、disturb = かき乱す。

questions.stem: Despite a difficult childhood, she ( ) to become a successful entrepreneur.
questions.explanation: go on to do = 〜することへと進む/〜するに至る (人の人生・キャリアの軌跡)。

questions.stem: The teacher's ( ) approach helped students gradually build confidence in their abilities.
questions.explanation: nurturing approach = 育成的なアプローチ。narrative = 物語の、narrow = 狭い、nautical = 航海の。

questions.stem: The new policy aims to ( ) longstanding inequalities in healthcare access.
questions.explanation: redress inequalities = 不平等を是正する。reduce は単に減らす (近いがニュアンスが弱い)、retreat = 後退、retain = 保持。

questions.stem: The presentation was ( ) thorough, leaving no question unanswered.
questions.explanation: impressively thorough = 印象的なほど徹底的。intimately = 親密に、ideologically = イデオロギー的に、invariably = 必ず。

questions.stem: The negotiations finally produced an agreement ( ) to all parties.
questions.explanation: acceptable to all parties = すべての当事者に受け入れ可能な (定型コロケーション)。accidental = 偶然、accustomed = 慣れた、accumulated = 蓄積された。

questions.stem: The author skillfully ( ) the historical period through detailed character studies.
questions.explanation: evoke a period / atmosphere = 時代/雰囲気を呼び起こす (文学評論頻出)。evict = 立ち退かせる、evade = 逃れる、evaluate = 評価。

questions.stem: The diplomat displayed remarkable ( ) in handling the complex negotiations.
questions.explanation: composure = 冷静さ/平静 (外交・難局頻出)。composer = 作曲家、composition = 構成、composite = 合成物。

questions.stem: The committee's recommendations were ( ) accepted by the board.
questions.explanation: promptly accepted = 速やかに受け入れられた (ビジネス文脈頻出)。precisely = 正確に、perpetually = 永続的に、peripherally = 周辺的に。

questions.stem: The professor's research has ( ) significant attention from international scholars.
questions.explanation: garner attention = 注目を集める (= attract attention のフォーマル版)。gain は単に得る、grant = 与える、glimpse = チラッと見る。

questions.stem: The company's financial difficulties have been ( ) by recent market conditions.
questions.explanation: compound difficulties = 困難をさらに増す/悪化させる (経済評論頻出)。complicate = 複雑化、complete = 完成、compress = 圧縮。

questions.stem: The artist's later works demonstrate a ( ) maturity in style and technique.
questions.explanation: new maturity = 新たな成熟 (定型コロケーション)。novelty = 新奇さ (名詞)、novel = 新奇な (形容詞だが書評では「画期的」のニュアンス)、novice = 初心者。

question_data.passage:
The Power of Walking

Walking is one of the simplest forms of human movement, requiring no special equipment, training, or facilities. For most of human history, walking was simply how people moved from place to place. Yet research now reveals that walking offers a remarkable array of benefits—benefits we are losing as automobile use, sedentary lifestyles, and urban designs that discourage walking become more common.

The physical benefits of walking are well-documented. Regular walking reduces risks of heart disease, type 2 diabetes, certain cancers, and many other conditions. It improves cardiovascular fitness and muscle strength. Compared to more strenuous exercise, walking has lower injury rates and can be sustained throughout life. Studies show that even modest amounts—30 minutes of brisk walking most days—produce substantial health benefits.

Mental and cognitive benefits are equally impressive. Walking outdoors has been shown to reduce symptoms of depression and anxiety. The combination of physical movement, exposure to natural light, and changing scenery appears to support mental health in ways that exercise alone or time outdoors alone may not. Research also suggests walking enhances creative thinking; many writers and thinkers have reported breakthroughs during walks.

Walking also serves social and community functions. Walking neighborhoods—where residents can comfortably walk to schools, shops, and services—tend to develop stronger social connections than auto-dependent areas. Casual encounters during walks, brief conversations with neighbors, shared participation in public spaces all contribute to community cohesion.

Despite these benefits, walking has been declining as a routine activity in many places. Urban designs prioritizing automobile traffic have made many areas physically dangerous or unpleasant for walking. Modern lifestyles often involve so much sitting—at work, during commutes, during leisure—that walking becomes an effortful exception rather than a daily habit. The cumulative effect on public health has been substantial.

Responses are emerging. Some cities have invested heavily in pedestrian infrastructure—wider sidewalks, traffic calming, walkable mixed-use neighborhoods. Health programs increasingly emphasize incorporating walking into daily routines. Walking groups provide both exercise and social benefits.

Reclaiming walking is not difficult in principle, though it requires deliberate choices in environments that often work against it. The benefits—for individual health, mental wellbeing, social connection, and environmental sustainability—are substantial enough to justify these efforts. Few activities offer such broad benefits at such low cost.

questions.stem: What benefits of walking does the passage describe?
choices: Physical (heart, diabetes, cancers prevention), mental (depression, anxiety reduction), cognitive (creativity), social (community connections)
questions.explanation: 第2-4段落で 4 つの利点 (physical / mental / cognitive / social) が論じられる。

questions.stem: What modest amount of walking produces substantial health benefits?
choices: 30 minutes of brisk walking most days
questions.explanation: 第2段落「even modest amounts—30 minutes of brisk walking most days—produce substantial health benefits」が根拠。

questions.stem: What does the author say about reclaiming walking?
choices: Not difficult in principle but requires deliberate choices; benefits substantial enough to justify efforts
questions.explanation: 最終段落「Reclaiming walking is not difficult in principle, though it requires deliberate choices ... benefits ... are substantial enough to justify these efforts」が根拠。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Will artificial intelligence replace many human jobs in the future?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Automation - New jobs - Inequality - Skills

Requirements: 120-150 words.
question_data.sample:
I believe AI will replace many human jobs, though the picture is more nuanced. I will support this view with two reasons: automation capabilities and resulting inequality.

First, current AI technology can already perform many tasks previously done by humans. Customer service, translation, basic content creation, paralegal work, and increasing portions of medical analysis are increasingly performed by AI systems. As these capabilities expand, more jobs will face automation pressure—including many that recently seemed safe.

Second, AI displacement may worsen economic inequality. Workers who lose jobs to AI may struggle to find equally well-paying alternatives, while those owning AI systems capture economic benefits. Without active policy responses—retraining programs, social safety nets, perhaps shorter working hours—the social consequences could be severe.

In conclusion, AI's job impacts will be substantial. The challenge is ensuring this transformation produces broadly shared benefits rather than concentrated advantages. (148 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 120-150 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準1級】立場明示+nuance、2ポイント (Automation + Inequality)・現代社会の論証・結論で政策的視点。

questions.stem: My brother enjoys ( ) novels in his free time.
questions.explanation: enjoy + 動名詞 = 〜することを楽しむ (enjoy reading は定型)。

questions.stem: The teacher gave us ( ) advice about studying.
questions.explanation: some + 形容詞 + 不可算名詞 (advice)。

questions.stem: This medicine should be taken ( ) meals.
questions.explanation: before meals = 食前に。ago は時間 (副詞)、past は過ぎて、when は接続詞。

questions.stem: The presentation was ( ) the best I have ever seen.
questions.explanation: by far the best = 群を抜いて最高 (最上級強調・定型句)。

questions.stem: I usually work out ( ) school.
questions.explanation: after school = 放課後 (定型)。later = もっと遅く、behind = 後ろに、next = 次に。

questions.stem: My grandmother taught me ( ) play the piano.
questions.explanation: teach + 人 + how to do = 人に〜の仕方を教える。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should students take part-time jobs while studying?

Requirements: 80-100 words with 2 reasons.
question_data.sample:
I think students should take part-time jobs while studying. I have two reasons.

First, part-time jobs help students develop important life skills. Working teaches time management, communication, responsibility, and how to interact with people from different backgrounds. These skills will benefit students throughout their lives, not just in their future careers.

Second, part-time jobs provide practical experience and earnings. Students learn what working life is like before committing to a career. The income helps them gain financial independence and reduces the burden on their families.

For these reasons, balanced part-time work alongside studies benefits students. (98 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 80-100 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級新形式】立場明示+2理由 (life skills + practical experience)・各補強・結論。

questions.stem: The library closes ( ) 8 PM on weekdays.
questions.explanation: at + 時刻 = 〜時に。on は曜日/日付、in は月/年、to は方向。

questions.stem: I often meet my friends ( ) Saturdays.
questions.explanation: on + 曜日 (Saturdays = 毎週土曜)。

questions.stem: My sister can speak three languages ( ).
questions.explanation: 動詞 speak を修飾するので副詞 fluently。

questions.stem: There are ( ) students in my class.
questions.explanation: 基数 thirty = 30。thirties は (10年単位の)「30 代」、thirtieth は序数 (30番目)、thirty's はアポストロフィ用法。

questions.stem: My father has been working ( ) this company for 20 years.
questions.explanation: work for + 会社 = 〜社で働く (定型)。at + 場所、in + 場所/分野。for が最自然。

questions.stem: Yumi is the ( ) of three sisters.
questions.explanation: 3 人中で「最も年下」 → 最上級 youngest。

questions.stem: He has ( ) been to New York three times.
questions.explanation: already = もう (現在完了肯定文で完了済み)。yet は否定/疑問、still は継続、ever は経験 (have ever been to の形)。

questions.stem: The professor explained the topic ( ) we could understand.
questions.explanation: so that S V = S V するように (目的の接続詞)。

questions.stem: It is not until you finish high school ( ) you can apply for college.
questions.explanation: It is not until X that S V = X してはじめて S V (定型強調構文)。準2級でも出題範囲。

question_data.passage:
Music in Modern Life

Music accompanies daily life in modern societies more than ever before. Streaming services place enormous music libraries in pockets and homes. Headphones make music available during commutes, exercise, and quiet moments at home. Background music plays in stores, restaurants, gyms, and many other spaces. The total amount of music humans now consume far exceeds anything previous generations experienced.

Research on the effects of pervasive music suggests both benefits and concerns. Music has powerful effects on mood, often shifting it deliberately or unconsciously. People use music to relax, energize, focus, or process emotions. These uses can substantially improve daily wellbeing.

Yet pervasive music also raises questions. Music intended for one purpose—say, relaxing background music in stores—may unconsciously affect listeners in ways they did not choose. Constant musical accompaniment may interfere with the kind of focused attention some activities require. The skills of really listening to music carefully may diminish when music becomes constant background.

Music's social functions have also changed. Earlier eras had music primarily as social experiences—people listening together, performing for each other, sharing songs at gatherings. Modern music consumption is often solitary, with each person inside their own headphone bubble. Some scholars worry that this shift, while individually convenient, has reduced music's traditional role in building social bonds.

Music education has not kept pace with music's expanded role. Many young people consume vast amounts of music while learning relatively little about how music works—musical theory, history, performance, composition. The capacity to engage thoughtfully with music differs from mere consumption of it.

Responses to these patterns vary. Some advocate intentional music listening—choosing what to hear deliberately rather than letting algorithms decide. Some emphasize learning to play instruments or sing as part of well-rounded education. Some recommend periods without any music at all, allowing the mind quieter conditions for reflection.

Music enriches modern life in ways previous generations could not have imagined. Yet how we engage with music shapes whether this richness produces deeper musical experience or merely more constant background noise. The choices we make individually and collectively will substantially shape how this remarkable cultural resource serves human lives.

questions.stem: What concerns does the passage raise about pervasive music?
choices: Music intended for one purpose may unconsciously affect listeners; constant accompaniment may interfere with focused attention; careful listening skills may diminish
questions.explanation: 第3段落「Music intended for one purpose ... may unconsciously affect listeners ... Constant musical accompaniment may interfere ... skills of really listening to music carefully may diminish」が根拠。

questions.stem: How has music's social function changed?
choices: Earlier eras had music as primarily social; modern consumption is often solitary—each person in headphone bubble
questions.explanation: 第4段落「Earlier eras had music primarily as social experiences ... Modern music consumption is often solitary, with each person inside their own headphone bubble」が根拠。

questions.stem: What does the author conclude about music in modern life?
choices: How we engage with music shapes whether this richness produces deeper experience or merely background noise; choices we make matter
questions.explanation: 最終段落「how we engage with music shapes whether this richness produces deeper musical experience or merely more constant background noise」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
=== Job Posting ===

Position: Junior Software Developer
Company: TechBridge Solutions
Location: Tokyo (Hybrid - 3 days office, 2 days remote)
Salary: ¥4.5M - ¥6M (annual)

Responsibilities:
• Develop and maintain web applications using JavaScript and Python
• Collaborate with senior developers on feature design
• Write clean, well-documented code
• Participate in code reviews and team meetings

Requirements:
• 1-3 years of programming experience
• Strong knowledge of JavaScript or Python
• Familiarity with version control (Git)
• Bachelor's degree in Computer Science or related field (or equivalent experience)
• Conversational English (TOEIC 600+ preferred)

Benefits:
• Health insurance, paid leave, retirement plan
• Annual training budget (¥150,000)
• Modern office in Shibuya with free coffee/lunch
• Stock options after 6 months

How to Apply:
Send resume + portfolio to careers@techbridge.example
Interviews start October 15. Position fills quickly.

questions.stem: When does the application review begin?

question_data.audio_script:
[共通テスト Part 2型 - 短い対話]

Dialogue:
Woman: I'd like to return this jacket. The size is too big.
Man: Sure. Do you have your receipt?
Woman: Yes, here you are. I bought it last Tuesday.
Man: I'll process the refund. Would you like store credit or your money back?
Woman: Money back, please.

Question: What does the woman want?

questions.stem: What does the woman want?
questions.explanation: 女性発言「Money back, please」が根拠。

questions.stem: Why is the woman returning the jacket?
questions.explanation: 女性最初の発言「The size is too big」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[共通テスト Part 6型 - 議論]

Four students discussing whether to keep face-to-face classes after pandemic restrictions ended.

Kenji: I think online classes are better in many ways. We saved commute time and could attend from anywhere.

Maya: But I missed face-to-face interaction. It's harder to ask questions in chat than in person, and group projects work better when we meet directly.

Takashi: Both have advantages. Maybe a hybrid—some classes online, some in person?

Sarah: That makes sense to me. Theoretical lectures could be online, but discussions and labs should be in person.

Kenji: I agree with the hybrid idea. The key is choosing the right format for each type of class.

questions.stem: What does Kenji initially favor?
questions.explanation: Kenji 冒頭「I think online classes are better in many ways」が根拠。

questions.stem: What does Maya prefer?
choices: Face-to-face for interaction and group projects
questions.explanation: Maya 発言「I missed face-to-face interaction ... group projects work better when we meet directly」が根拠。

questions.stem: What compromise do they reach?
choices: Hybrid: theoretical lectures online, discussions and labs in person
questions.explanation: Sarah 発言「Theoretical lectures could be online, but discussions and labs should be in person」と Kenji 同意「I agree with the hybrid idea」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
There is something paradoxical about how human beings handle uncertainty. On one hand, we develop sophisticated tools for reasoning under uncertainty—statistical methods, probability theory, decision theory—that have substantially improved human capacity to think well about uncertain matters. On the other hand, we routinely make poor decisions about uncertainty in ways that contradict the formal tools we have developed. We over-weight vivid possibilities while ignoring statistical patterns. We treat low-probability dramatic events as more likely than high-probability mundane ones. We become overconfident about the future while routinely surprised when it differs from our expectations.

This pattern has multiple sources. Cognitive systems evolved to navigate the practical risks of ancestral environments may not be well-adapted to statistical reasoning about modern circumstances. Vivid stories impress us more than abstract probabilities. Recent events feel more representative than carefully analyzed historical data. Group dynamics encourage convergence on dominant views even when individual members harbor private doubts.

The consequences extend across many domains. Public reactions to terrorism, plane crashes, and other dramatic but statistically rare events often prompt policy responses substantially disproportionate to actual risk. Investment decisions are systematically distorted by emotional reactions to recent market movements. Medical decisions sometimes weight rare side effects heavily while underweighting more common but less vivid risks.

Responses to these patterns are difficult but not impossible. Education in statistical thinking and probabilistic reasoning helps individuals reason better about uncertain matters, though the cognitive biases involved are robust enough to persist even in those trained to recognize them. Institutional structures can sometimes compensate for individual reasoning failures—for example, statistical algorithms in medical diagnosis sometimes outperform human judgment on similar cases. Cultural acknowledgment that uncertainty is genuinely difficult to reason about may produce more humble decision-making than confident assertions in either direction.

The alternative—pretending we can simply think clearly about uncertainty without effort—has substantial costs. Decisions made on the basis of vivid intuitions rather than careful analysis tend toward systematic errors. Public policies premised on clear thinking about uncertain matters often go badly when the thinking turns out to be much more error-prone than assumed. Acknowledging genuine difficulties of reasoning under uncertainty seems essential to navigating them better.

questions.stem: 下線部 'Cognitive systems evolved to navigate the practical risks of ancestral environments may not be well-adapted to statistical reasoning about modern circumstances.' を日本語に訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 祖先の環境における実際的なリスクを切り抜けるために進化した認知システムは、現代の状況に関する統計的推論には十分に適応していないのかもしれない。
questions.explanation: 【東大型】① Cognitive systems evolved to do = 〜するために進化した認知システム (過去分詞 evolved の限定修飾 + to不定詞) ② navigate the practical risks of A = A の実際的リスクを切り抜ける ③ ancestral environments = 祖先の環境 (進化心理学用語) ④ may not be well-adapted to X = X に十分に適応していないかもしれない ⑤ statistical reasoning about modern circumstances = 現代の状況に関する統計的推論。【ポイント】evolved to do の現在分詞的限定修飾、be adapted to の受動的形容詞用法。

questions.stem: What broader argument does the author make about uncertainty?
choices: Acknowledging genuine difficulties of reasoning under uncertainty is essential to navigating them better; humility produces more accurate decisions
questions.explanation: 最終段落「Acknowledging genuine difficulties of reasoning under uncertainty seems essential to navigating them better」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
Among the more curious patterns in the history of ideas is how innovations that initially seem clearly correct often turn out to require substantial revision. Sometimes the revisions are minor, refining details while leaving the basic insight intact. Other times the revisions undermine the original innovation almost entirely. The pattern suggests that even our most confident judgments about which ideas deserve continued belief often prove unreliable on longer timescales.

Consider economic theory. The classical economic insights of Adam Smith—the productivity gains from specialization, the coordinating power of markets, the limits of central planning—remain enormously influential. Yet later economists substantially revised many specific Smithian claims. The 'invisible hand' has been understood with much greater nuance than Smith proposed. Economic externalities require deliberate policy intervention that pure markets often handle poorly. Recurring market crises reveal limitations in models assuming continuous rational adjustment.

Similar patterns appear in physics. Newtonian mechanics, which seemed for two centuries to provide a complete account of physical reality, turned out to be a special case of more general theories—relativity at high speeds and large scales, quantum mechanics at small scales. The original framework was not simply wrong but was incomplete in ways that required fundamental theoretical revision.

Medicine provides similar examples. Many treatments once considered standard practice turned out to be ineffective or even harmful when tested rigorously. Theories about disease causation have undergone repeated revision as new evidence emerges. The history of medicine shows that confident professional consensus sometimes proves wrong in ways that affect patient outcomes substantially.

This pattern suggests intellectual humility about contemporary confident beliefs. The frameworks that today's experts confidently apply will likely require some revision as new evidence and new perspectives emerge. The specific nature of those revisions is impossible to predict in advance.

None of this argues for skepticism about all current knowledge. Some claims are better grounded than others, and our best current understanding usually represents genuine progress over earlier views. The argument is rather that progress involves continuing revision, that even the most confident current beliefs may require modification, and that intellectual maturity involves holding views with appropriate provisional confidence rather than absolute certainty.

questions.stem: 下線部 'progress involves continuing revision' を日本語に訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 進歩とは絶え間ない改訂を含むものなのである。
questions.explanation: 【京大型】① progress = 進歩 (主語) ② involves = 含む/伴う ③ continuing revision = 絶え間ない改訂/継続的な改訂 (現在分詞 continuing の限定修飾)。短文だが nuance を伝える訳出が要点。【別訳】「進歩は継続的な改訂を必要とする」「進歩とは絶え間ない見直しを伴うものである」など。

questions.stem: What examples does the passage cite of innovations requiring revision?
choices: Smith's economic theory (later refined), Newtonian mechanics (special case of relativity/quantum), medical treatments (sometimes ineffective when tested)
questions.explanation: 第2-4段落で 3 つの例が論じられる。

question_data.passage:
The Future of Workplaces

The workplace, as a place where employees physically gather to perform their jobs, is undergoing fundamental transformation. The pandemic-era shift to remote work demonstrated possibilities that many companies had previously dismissed. Some have embraced permanent remote arrangements, others have insisted on returns to offices, and most have arrived at various hybrid models. The implications for productivity, collaboration, urban life, and personal wellbeing are still being worked out.

Productivity research suggests that remote work can match or exceed office-based productivity for many tasks, particularly those requiring focused individual work. Distractions are different at home, but the specific distractions of office environments—interruptions, meetings, ambient noise—do not exist in the same forms remotely. Many workers report being more productive at home, though this varies considerably by task and personality.

Collaboration presents more complex pictures. Some collaborative tasks work surprisingly well through video calls and shared documents. Others—creative brainstorming, mentorship of junior colleagues, building deep professional relationships—seem to benefit from physical presence in ways video cannot fully replicate. The optimal balance varies by task and team, with no single model serving all situations equally well.

Worker preferences strongly favor flexibility. Surveys consistently show employees value the ability to work from home at least part of the time. Companies that have eliminated this flexibility have sometimes faced talent retention difficulties. The competitive labor market pressure increasingly forces employers to offer some remote work options.

Urban implications are substantial. Office buildings in many city centers are experiencing reduced occupancy rates with consequences extending to public transit ridership, restaurants and services that depended on office workers, and the broader economic ecosystem of business districts. Some cities are adapting by encouraging conversion of office buildings to residential use; others struggle to redefine their core economic functions.

Long-term consequences remain uncertain. Some predicted shifts—mass departures from cities, complete office abandonment—have not occurred at the scale once forecast. Yet substantial change has clearly happened, with effects continuing to unfold. The workplace transformation likely represents permanent shifts in how work and physical location relate, even though specific outcomes vary across industries, companies, and regions.

questions.stem: What does productivity research suggest about remote work?
choices: Remote work can match or exceed office productivity for many tasks, particularly focused individual work
questions.explanation: 第2段落「remote work can match or exceed office-based productivity for many tasks, particularly those requiring focused individual work」が根拠。

questions.stem: What kind of work seems to benefit from physical presence?
choices: Creative brainstorming, mentorship of junior colleagues, building deep professional relationships
questions.explanation: 第3段落「creative brainstorming, mentorship of junior colleagues, building deep professional relationships—seem to benefit from physical presence」が根拠。

questions.stem: What does the author conclude about workplace transformation?
choices: Substantial change has happened representing permanent shifts in how work and physical location relate, though specifics vary
questions.explanation: 最終段落「workplace transformation likely represents permanent shifts in how work and physical location relate, even though specific outcomes vary」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Economics of Innovation

Why do some societies produce continuous streams of innovations that transform technology, business, and daily life, while others remain technologically static? The question has long fascinated economic historians and remains highly relevant for contemporary policy debates about how to foster innovation in modern economies.

Classical economics has tended to view innovation as outside the system being analyzed—an external factor that arrives unpredictably and reshapes economic relationships. More recent endogenous growth theory has tried to bring innovation inside the analytical framework, seeking to understand what conditions encourage innovative activity within economies.

Several factors appear consistently associated with innovation. Strong intellectual property protection encourages investment in research and development by ensuring that innovators capture some of the value they create. Substantial public investment in basic research provides foundational scientific knowledge that private firms can later develop. Educational systems that produce both technical specialists and broadly capable graduates contribute substantial human capital. Geographic concentrations of related expertise—technology clusters, university towns, financial centers—accelerate knowledge transfer through informal contacts.

Yet these factors are necessary rather than sufficient. Many countries with similar policy regimes show very different innovation outcomes. Cultural factors—attitudes toward risk-taking, treatment of business failure, social mobility—seem to matter, though they are difficult to study rigorously. Path dependence makes innovation patterns somewhat self-reinforcing; existing innovations attract investment in further innovations in adjacent areas.

The relationship between competition and innovation is more complex than simple stories suggest. Some monopoly power may be necessary for substantial R&D investment, since perfectly competitive firms cannot capture enough value to justify research costs. Yet excessive market power often reduces innovation pressure, allowing dominant firms to delay rather than develop new technologies. The optimal balance varies by industry and historical moment.

Government policy intersects with innovation in multiple ways. Direct funding of basic research, tax incentives for R&D, regulatory frameworks affecting new technologies, and competition policy all influence innovation outcomes. The specific design of these policies matters more than the overall level of government involvement; well-designed limited interventions often outperform substantial but poorly-targeted ones.

For contemporary economies seeking to maintain or accelerate innovation, several lessons emerge. Sustained investment in research and education matters for outcomes years and decades later. Maintaining competitive but not destructive competition allows continued innovation incentive. Allowing failure—of individual ventures and even entire industries—frees resources for newer activities. Trying to predict and direct specific innovations rarely works as well as creating conditions where many innovations can be tried.

questions.stem: What factors does research find associated with innovation?
choices: Strong IP protection, public investment in basic research, educational systems, geographic concentrations of expertise
questions.explanation: 第3段落「Strong intellectual property protection ... Substantial public investment in basic research ... Educational systems ... Geographic concentrations of related expertise」が根拠。慶應経済型は経済論文の構造化情報把握が頻出。

questions.stem: What is complex about competition's relationship to innovation?
choices: Some monopoly power may be necessary for R&D investment, but excessive market power reduces innovation pressure
questions.explanation: 第5段落「Some monopoly power may be necessary for substantial R&D investment ... Yet excessive market power often reduces innovation pressure」が根拠。

questions.stem: What lessons does the author draw for contemporary economies?
choices: Sustained R&D/education investment, competitive but not destructive competition, allowing failure, creating conditions for many innovations
questions.explanation: 最終段落「Sustained investment in research and education ... Maintaining competitive but not destructive competition ... Allowing failure ... creating conditions where many innovations can be tried」が根拠。

question_data.passage: 東大理系数学 大問1 (9)

questions.stem: 次の極限値を求めよ: lim(x→0) (e^x - 1)/x
questions.answer:
これは有名な極限値で、e^x の x = 0 での微分係数に等しい。

方法 1 (Taylor 展開):
e^x = 1 + x + x²/2! + x³/3! + ...
e^x - 1 = x + x²/2! + x³/3! + ...
(e^x - 1)/x = 1 + x/2 + x²/6 + ...
x → 0 で → 1

方法 2 (ロピタルの定理):
lim (e^x - 1)/x → 形 0/0
ロピタルの定理: lim e^x / 1 = e⁰ / 1 = 1

方法 3 (微分の定義):
(e^x - 1)/x = (e^x - e⁰)/(x - 0) → 関数 f(x) = e^x の x = 0 における微分係数 = e⁰ = 1

∴ lim(x→0) (e^x - 1)/x = 1
questions.explanation: 【東大型】① 重要極限 lim(x→0)(e^x-1)/x = 1 ② 方法は Taylor / ロピタル / 微分の定義 から選択 ③ 関連: lim(x→0)(sin x)/x = 1、lim(x→0)(1-cos x)/x² = 1/2、lim(x→0)(ln(1+x))/x = 1。

question_data.passage: 東大理系物理 大問1 (9)

questions.stem: 電磁波の速度を c = 1/√(ε₀μ₀) とする。真空中での光速 c の値を求めよ (ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² F/m, μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m)。
questions.answer:
c = 1/√(ε₀ μ₀)

ε₀ μ₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² × 4π × 10⁻⁷
      = 8.85 × 4π × 10⁻¹⁹
      ≈ 8.85 × 12.566 × 10⁻¹⁹
      ≈ 111.2 × 10⁻¹⁹
      ≈ 1.112 × 10⁻¹⁷ s²/m²

c = 1/√(1.112 × 10⁻¹⁷)
  = 1/(1.055 × 10⁻⁸·⁵)
  ≈ 1/(3.336 × 10⁻⁹)
  ≈ 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s

∴ c ≈ 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s (= 30 万 km/s)

[歴史] マクスウェルが電磁気の方程式から導いた電磁波の速度が、当時測定されていた光速と一致したことから、光は電磁波の一種であると判明 (1860 年代)。
questions.explanation: 【東大型】① 真空中の光速 c = 1/√(ε₀μ₀) ② ε₀ (真空誘電率) と μ₀ (真空透磁率) の積から導出 ③ c ≈ 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s = 30 万 km/s。【物理史】光が電磁波であることの発見はマクスウェル方程式から数学的に導かれた驚異的な結果。

question_data.passage: 東大理系化学 大問1 (9)

questions.stem: 標準状態 (0°C, 1 atm) で、(1) 気体 1 mol の体積、(2) 1 g の H₂ ガスの体積を求めよ。
questions.answer:
(1) 標準状態の気体 1 mol の体積:
V = nRT/P
気体定数 R = 0.082 atm·L/(mol·K)、T = 273 K、P = 1 atm
V = 1.0 × 0.082 × 273 / 1.0 = 22.4 L

つまり標準状態の気体 1 mol = 22.4 L (これは物理量として記憶しておく)。

(2) 1 g の H₂ の体積:
H₂ のモル質量 = 2.0 g/mol
物質量 n = 1.0 / 2.0 = 0.50 mol

V = 0.50 × 22.4 = 11.2 L

∴ (1) 22.4 L、(2) 11.2 L

[補足] アボガドロの法則 (1811): 同温・同圧の気体は同数の分子を含む = 同じ体積。気体の種類によらず 1 mol = 22.4 L (標準状態)。
questions.explanation: 【東大型】① 標準状態 1 mol = 22.4 L (= モル体積、Vₘ) ② アボガドロの法則 (気体の種類によらず) ③ PV = nRT の R = 0.082 atm·L/(mol·K) または 8.31 J/(mol·K)。

question_data.passage: 京大理系数学 大問1 (9)

questions.stem: x²+ y² = 25 を満たす整数解 (x, y) をすべて求めよ (整数 x, y はそれぞれ ±)。
questions.explanation: 【京大型整数論】① x² + y² = n² の整数解 ② ピタゴラス数 (3, 4, 5) を覚える ③ 各 x で y² が完全平方数になるかチェック ④ 計 12 個 = (4 軸方向 0°/90°/180°/270° × 1) + (8 ピタゴラス点)。

question_data.passage: 京大理系物理 大問1 (9)

questions.stem: 質量 m の単振り子 (糸の長さ L) について、振動の周期 T を求めよ (重力加速度 g、振幅は小さいとする)。
questions.answer:
小さな振幅 (sin θ ≈ θ) の単振り子の運動方程式:
m L (d²θ/dt²) = -mg sin θ ≈ -mg θ
→ d²θ/dt² = -(g/L) θ

単振動の方程式 d²θ/dt² = -ω²θ から ω² = g/L。

角振動数 ω = √(g/L)
周期 T = 2π/ω = 2π √(L/g)

∴ T = 2π √(L/g)

[興味深い特性]
- 周期は質量 m に依存しない! (振り子の質量を変えても周期は同じ → ガリレオ・ガリレイの発見)
- 重力加速度 g に依存する → 振り子で g の測定可能 (重力計)
- 長さ L が長いほど周期が長い (4 倍 L で周期 2 倍)

[応用例] 振り子時計の精度の基礎、地震計、惑星探査での重力測定。
questions.explanation: 【京大型】① 単振り子の周期 T = 2π√(L/g) ② 質量に依存しない (= 自由落下と同じ理由) ③ ガリレオが発見した有名な周期の独立性。【関連】ばね振り子 T = 2π√(m/k) と対比 (こちらは m に依存)。

question_data.passage: 国公立医学部化学 大問1 (7)

questions.stem: タンパク質の四次構造とは何か説明せよ。具体例として、ヘモグロビンの構造と機能の関係を述べよ。
questions.answer:
タンパク質の構造の階層:
- 一次構造: アミノ酸配列 (一次元の文字列)
- 二次構造: α ヘリックス、β シート (局所的な水素結合パターン)
- 三次構造: 1 本のポリペプチド鎖の三次元的折りたたみ (テルティアリ)
- 四次構造 (Quaternary structure): 複数のポリペプチド鎖 (サブユニット) が集合した複合体

四次構造を持つタンパク質: 複数のサブユニットが特定の配置で集合し、機能を発揮する。

ヘモグロビン (Hb) の例:
- 4 つのサブユニットから構成 (α 鎖 2 本 + β 鎖 2 本 = α₂β₂ 構造)
- 各サブユニットに 1 つのヘム基 (鉄イオン Fe²⁺ を中心とするポルフィリン環) が結合
- 4 つの O₂ 分子と協同的に結合

協同性 (cooperativity):
1 番目の O₂ が 1 つのヘムに結合すると、他のサブユニットの構造変化を誘発し、残りのヘムの O₂ 結合性が高まる (アロステリック効果)。
→ 結果としてヘモグロビンの O₂ 結合曲線は S 字型 (シグモイド曲線) になる。

この協同性が血液による効率的な O₂ 運搬を可能にする:
- 肺 (高 O₂ 分圧): O₂ 飽和率高 (~98%)
- 末梢組織 (低 O₂ 分圧): O₂ を放出 (~30%)
- ミオグロビン (1 サブユニットのみ・協同性なし) は双曲線型結合曲線 → 筋中の O₂ 貯蔵に最適
questions.explanation: 【医学部生化学】① タンパク質構造の階層 (一次〜四次) ② ヘモグロビン α₂β₂ 構造、4 つのヘム ③ 協同性 / アロステリック効果による S 字型結合曲線 ④ 効率的な O₂ 運搬のメカニズム。【病態】鎌状赤血球症 (sickle cell anemia) は β 鎖 6 番目アミノ酸 (Glu→Val) の一次構造変化が四次構造・機能を破壊する古典例。

question_data.passage: 阪大理系物理 大問1 (7)

questions.stem: 超音波と人間の聴覚の関係について、(1) 人間が聞こえる周波数範囲、(2) 超音波の応用例 を 3 つ述べよ。
questions.answer:
(1) 人間の可聴周波数範囲:
約 20 Hz - 20,000 Hz (= 20 kHz)

下限以下: 超低周波 (infrasound、地震・大型動物の鳴き声・気象現象)
上限以上: 超音波 (ultrasound、20 kHz 以上)

年齢で聴こえる範囲は変化 (高齢ほど高周波が聞こえなくなる、特に 8 kHz 以上)。

(2) 超音波の応用例 (3 つ):

① 医療超音波エコー (診断):
- 周波数: 1-15 MHz
- 体内で反射する超音波から組織断面像を生成
- 妊娠中の胎児観察、心臓 (心エコー)、腹部臓器、血流評価
- 非侵襲・放射線なし・即時画像化

② 超音波洗浄 (cleaning):
- 液体中で超音波が起こす微小な空洞 (cavitation) で汚れを物理的に剥離
- 精密機械部品、医療器具、宝飾品、メガネの洗浄

③ 距離測定・物体検出 (sonar / 超音波センサー):
- 超音波の発信→反射→受信時間で距離計算
- 自動車のバック警告、産業用 (工場でのワーク検出)、海洋探査 (魚群探知機・海底地形)
- コウモリ・イルカは生物的にこれを使う (反響定位 echolocation)

その他の応用: 超音波カミソリ・歯石除去 (歯科)、肝臓癌の超音波焼却治療、ピエゾ素子による超音波発生・受信。
questions.explanation: 【阪大型】① 可聴域 20 Hz - 20 kHz (年齢で減少) ② 超音波 = 20 kHz 以上 ③ 医療診断、洗浄、距離測定が代表応用。【医学応用】超音波エコー検査は放射線被ばくなく安全 (= 妊娠中も使える)、心エコー (echocardiogram) で心臓機能評価、ドップラーで血流速度測定。

question_data.passage: 国公立医学部物理 大問1 (7)

questions.stem: X 線について、(1) 性質と医療応用、(2) 被ばく単位 (Gy, Sv) の意味 を述べよ。
questions.answer:
(1) X 線の性質と医療応用:

性質:
- 電磁波 (光と同じ・周波数 10¹⁶ - 10²⁰ Hz、波長 0.01 - 10 nm)
- 高エネルギー光子 (KeV - MeV)
- 物質を透過する能力 (= 透過力)、特に密度の低い組織 (水・脂肪)
- 重元素 (Ca, Fe, ヨウ素造影剤等) で吸収される
- DNA 分子に損傷を与える (= 電離放射線)

医療応用:
- 単純 X 線写真 (胸部・骨折の評価)
- CT スキャン (断面像の重ね合わせで 3D)
- 透視 (リアルタイム動画)
- 造影 X 線検査 (バリウム消化管撮影、血管造影)
- 放射線治療 (がん細胞の DNA 破壊)

(2) 被ばく単位:

吸収線量 [グレイ Gy = J/kg]:
- 物質 (体組織) 1 kg が吸収したエネルギー (J)
- 単純な物理量 (組織の種類による違いを考慮しない)

等価線量・実効線量 [シーベルト Sv]:
- Gy に放射線種別の生物学的効果係数 (W_R) を掛けたもの
- W_R: X線 1、α線 20、中性子 5-20
- 異なる種類の放射線の生物影響を比較できる

例: 胸部 X 線 1 枚 ≈ 0.1 mSv、CT スキャン ≈ 5-10 mSv、地球の自然放射線 ≈ 2 mSv/年、放射線治療 ≈ 60 Gy (局所、患者あたり)。

医療被ばくは正当化 (検査価値) と最適化 (必要最小量) の原則で管理される (ALARA: As Low As Reasonably Achievable)。
questions.explanation: 【医学部物理】① X 線 = 電磁波、透過力高、DNA 損傷力あり ② 用途: 撮影 (CT/X 線写真)、治療 (放射線治療) ③ Gy = 物理量 (吸収エネルギー / 質量)、Sv = 生物学的効果考慮量。【法令】医療被ばく ALARA 原則、職業被ばくの上限 (放射線科医・技師)。

question_data.passage: 慶大理工生物 大問1 (5)

questions.stem: 生物の進化における自然選択 (natural selection) について、(1) 提唱者と原理 (2) 進化の過程と現代的意義 を述べよ。
questions.answer:
(1) 自然選択の提唱者と原理:

提唱者: チャールズ・ダーウィン (Charles Darwin) と アルフレッド・ラッセル・ウォレス (Alfred Russel Wallace)、独立に同時期 (1858 年)。
ダーウィンの著書「種の起源」(On the Origin of Species, 1859) で詳述。

原理 (4 つの基本要素):
① 変異 (variation): 同一種内でも個体ごとに形質が異なる (遺伝・突然変異が源)
② 過剰繁殖 (overproduction): 生物は環境が支えられる以上の子孫を産む
③ 生存競争 (struggle for existence): 限られた資源で個体間の競争が生じる
④ 自然選択 (natural selection): 環境への適応度の高い個体が生存・繁殖し、その形質が次世代に伝わる

ダーウィンの偉大さは、これら 4 要素を統合的に理解した点。

(2) 進化の過程と現代的意義:

進化のメカニズム (現代的解釈):
- 突然変異により遺伝的変異が継続的に生じる
- 自然選択により有利な形質が頻度上昇
- 遺伝的浮動 (genetic drift) により小集団でランダムに変異固定
- 種分化 (speciation) により新種が誕生

現代的意義:
- 生物進化全体の統一的理論的基礎 (modern synthesis: ダーウィン進化論 + メンデル遺伝学 + 集団遺伝学)
- 医学への応用: 抗生物質耐性菌の進化 (= 自然選択の臨床的事例)、がん細胞の進化 (= 治療抵抗性獲得)、ウイルスの進化 (= COVID-19 変異株)
- 生命科学全般の基盤 (= 「生物学は進化なくして意味をなさない」、ドブジャンスキー)

[現代の挑戦] 抗生物質耐性、農薬耐性害虫、化学療法抵抗性がん細胞 — すべて自然選択の高速版。
questions.explanation: 【慶應理工生物 / 医学部】① ダーウィン + ウォレスの自然選択 (1858) ② 4 要素: 変異 / 過剰繁殖 / 生存競争 / 自然選択 ③ 現代的意義: 抗生物質耐性等の医学的事例。
